speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is October 15, 1996. Our survivor is Arnold Greenblatt. I'm the interviewer, Mark Bernstein. We are conducting the interview in Brooklyn, New York, USA. And our language is English. Today's date is October 15, 1996. I have the pleasure of interviewing Mr. Arnold Greenblatt. I am the interviewer, Mark Bernstein. And we are conducting the interview in Brooklyn, New York, USA. And our language is English. Could you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arnold Greenblatt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-R-double E-N-B-L-A- double T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: A-R-N-O-L-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Yosef Dovid Ben Yehudah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 11, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 68-- 67, I'll be 68 in January.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Debrecen, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home and what outside your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home Hungarian and Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And outside home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside home, Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Ignatz Greenblatt. My mother's name was Sarah Greenblatt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was born in [NON-ENGLISH], a small town in Hungary. My mother was born in [NON-ENGLISH], which is not too far from that same city, town in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell those and tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I couldn't tell you. I wouldn't know how to spell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where are they in Hungary? What part of Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Satmar. It's that area called Satmar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was a tailor. He learned from his father. He worked with about 35 people as a wholesaler in men's suits. And just before the war broke out, he came to America. I was at the age of 12 when he left.

speaker: interviewer
text: He went on his own?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came-- he came by himself, because my grandmother, my mother's mother, was in America. And my mother pressured him to-- we should all go to America, you know, being that what was happening in Europe, you know, to get away from it. And he said, I have America here. And he decided my mother wants to come so badly, so he left to America. And then the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your father before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I remember about my father that he was a very charitable man, a very religious man. When it came-- what's that holiday we have-- Hanukkah-- he would come to our school and measure the needy children for suits and coats and things, and he would give them for Hanukkah as-- as free clothing. A very good father, a very good provider, strict. Every Friday night he would call me and set me in his lap, and ask me what I learned during the week, in cheder, when I learned Yiddish and Hebrew. Well, he-- I can't say nuthin' bad about him. He was a great man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother also, a very charitable person, very religious. She used to tell my father every Friday night, when we went to shul, to bring somebody home for supper. You know, [NON-ENGLISH], somebody that had no family in the city. And he did so. I remember it quite well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents well off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents well off financially?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. We had a sleep-in maid, until the Jews were not allowed to employ Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name in Hungarian was Bushke. That's, I think, Betty it's called you know, in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I was a young fellow, a little guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you or do you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have one brother and two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me their names and their age difference to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest sister is 73. Her name is Clara-- her married name? T-H-A-W, Thaw. My brother's is-- my brother is Ernie Greenblatt. He is 70. He's three years older than I am. And my younger sister is Judy Stevens, is married name, who is a year younger than myself, which is 67, 66.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me a little bit about your brother and sister? [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my sister is a housewife. She has three children. Her husband passed away about five years ago, lives in Florida. My brother is in-- lives in New Jersey. He was in a dental business, dental lab. He's got two children, a boy and a girl. He's financially very stable. And he still comes into Brooklyn to work, at the age of 70, from New Jersey. He was the founder of my synagogue that I belong to, president at one time, charitable man, a good-hearted man, a good father. My youngest sister who lives in California, who is brokenhearted from what happened to our mother, with what we've been through-- she was a little girl. And she's a mother of two children, and a grandmother of two. And she still works.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about them from before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, my younger sister, I looked out for her, as the younger sister, from being attacked by the Gentiles comin' out of school. I protected her. And we had great love for each other. We still do. My older brother, my brother, he-- he was like a father to me when my dad left for America. Because I was the type of kid that didn't want to go to cheder. And when the anti-Semitism started, I cut off my payos, and I let my hair grow. And before that, I was more of-- of the Orthodox style. And he beat me up once in while [LAUGHS] as-- you know, as punishment for not going to cheder. But I still love him very dearly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live before the war? Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Debrecen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the town, your memories of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Debrecen is a fairly large city. It has trolley cars. It had public swimming pool in the summertime, for recreation, Olympic-sized pools out in the forest. It had great hospitals. Because I was in one of them, and they was going to operate on me, and I ran away. So I-- they thought that I had an appendix problem. And I still have my appendix, by the way. It was never taken out. I ran away. It has a large hotel, which I've been to recently. As a child, I used to admire people sitting outside reading the paper in front of the hotel. There was like an enclosure, so that to keep the public from interfering. And people were drinking beer or coffee. It's an interesting city, very nice. It was, anyway. As of now, I wouldn't want to live there anymore. But it's a nice city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of religious affiliation did your family have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of Jewish education did you receive? Who taught you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a rabbi. In those days we had rabbis that had their own school in their house, where they had 15-20 children. And then I had a private tutor. When I got caught not going to the-- to the rabbi. And my mother hired a private tutor to teach me [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Falkowicz was one. As a matter of fact, he was a very tough rabbi. He used a very, very straight stick. If you missed-- the other one, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember the name of the [NON-ENGLISH] who taught you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the shul you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: The shul I went to was the large orthodox shul, with a balcony on both-- or all around except the front, where the [NON-ENGLISH] was. But the women were upstairs, the men downstairs with the [NON-ENGLISH] in the center. They had about 35 [NON-ENGLISH], and when it came Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, I sang in a choir, I sang solo. I-- with our cantor who unfortunately was massacred by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Fishl Silber, the best chazzan that I ever heard in my life, up until today. [NON-ENGLISH], and nobody could go up against him. That's how great he was. We used to practice before yontif. My favorite yontif was [NON-ENGLISH], where with the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, they're going around with a Torah, with a procession that never ended. Shul was-- our shul was decorated with flowers that when you walked in, you thought you were in a garden somewhere. It was so beautiful. That was the year that was the shul that was. That was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people did the shul accommodate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say 500.

speaker: interviewer
text: Including the women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Including the women.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a rabbi of the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very, very old rabbi, with a long white beard. His name was Strasser.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I wouldn't know how to spell his name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A distinguished rabbi, and then there was the shamas, the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, that was all part of their employees of the shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only one I remember is the cantor, and the-- oh yes, yeah, the [NON-ENGLISH] Gross, the Yiddish name, I don't know. Because he has a son here that's a chazzan in the Forest Hills Jewish Center, Yeah, his name was Gross.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this rabbi was the rabbi that your father would ask [NON-ENGLISH] to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe a typical Shabbos for you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Typical Shabbos, yes. Typical Shabbos was, going way back when I was very young, my mother sent us to the mikvah. We came home, got dressed. And Friday night she light candles, and my father and my brother and I went to shul. In the early years, my younger years where we didn't have any hatred from the goyim, it was very pleasant. Shabbos, we went to shul. On the way home, we stopped at the bakery, picked up the [NON-ENGLISH]. The neighborhood smelled from when the baker opened the oven. All the children were coming out-- they could smell it all over the neighborhood. It was so nice. My mother baked challahs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was an eruv in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before you're 13, you-- you carry it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no eruv no. Because as a child I remember my cousin was born. And his bris was on Shabbos. And I had to carry the knife for the the mohel. You know, these things I remember like it was yesterday. And then, of course, they had the kiddush in the house. Shabbos was very nice. Friends come over, and they play. And as a matter of fact we had a little hallway like where I had my own little shul as-- as a child. I-- I was a very good Jew that time. I still am, you know. But it was-- it was a nice life, until the Nazis got stronger, and when we come out of shul, we used to got into fights, you know. They used to attack us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things would happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would beat you, you know, take sticks or stones, they throw at you. [BACKROUND NOISE] That was when the Nazis came in. Then the Hungarians, of course, sided with them, you know, the Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that happen before the Nazis came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That happened before, yes, the fascists, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was there-- were there incidents just on a less-organized level?

speaker: interviewee
text: Less-- as an individual level? Yes, there was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things would happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fights, you know, dirty Jew. If you had a payos, they pulled on it. I personally was stuck into a pile of snow head first. I was a-- I was a fighter. I never turned the other cheek, or run away from a good fight. I was a big boy for my age, and-- and very nervy, and I fought back, and especially if it had to do to protect my sister. You know, when she came out of school or she would be attacked, I was right there to protect her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about Pesach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pesach, I remember very well. I was-- before Pesach I was bitten by a dog. It was-- the matzah factory was in a basement of our school. And in Europe, matzah was a novelty, you know, when Pesach came. So we would, kids come out of school, we would go to the basement window and ask, can I have a piece of matzah? And this dog was roaming around, and somehow I kicked it or chased it. And the dog chased me, and caught me, and bit me. [LAUGHS] Pesach was a nice, nice yontif. My father had Seder, sit on a pillow, put on a kettle. As for that matter, not only Pesach, but every-- every Friday night, or Shabbos after a meal, we sat at the table, the table everybody together, and we sang [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, of course, I was asked to [NON-ENGLISH], because I was the younger. And it was something to look forward to. It was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of foods did your mother prepare for Shabbos and yontif?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], for one, for Shabbos. In the summertime, we used to have cold food, fruit soup, watermelons, summer fruits and things like that. And of course, the beautiful challahs she baked, cakes, kindl which was-- still my favorite. That if I see it, I can't turn away from it. And the maid used to go to [NON-ENGLISH] to check the-- the-- what do they call the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, for the-- for the ducks, to see if it's kosher, you know, if it's good. These are things-- I mean there's many things that-- that comes to me throughout the years. When I have my own Seder here with my children and grandchildren it brings back memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any religious customs in general that you remember from your town or family that you do not see so much nowadays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see. No. Well, basically the shul that I belong to is about the same. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's sukkah, where I always build the sukkah for my father. That was an exciting thing. No, not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big would you say the Jewish population was in your town, and how big the non-Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: By numbers, I wouldn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Percentage-wise?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Gentiles was a larger percentage, more-- more goyim than Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What percentage would you say roughly?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 70 and 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say the relationship was like in general between the Jews and non-Jews of your general area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had some good ones. I had non-Jewish friends that lived on the same street. As a matter of fact, they offered to hide me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Don't forget, it's over 50 years ago. So I don't remember their names. But I would not leave my mother and my sister and brother. Without me they would have never survived. And the relationship, in general, wasn't too bad, until the Germans came in and they tried to-- try to get what the Jews had. There were some good in 'em.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was some good. But there was a lot of them not good, bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of the schools you attended before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no particular name to the school. The school that I attended to learn Hungarian, you know, the Hungarian school, it belonged to the shul, to the synagogue. There was one particular block, where half of the block, the building was a four-story building. It was a Jewish school. And the the other school was a big wall separated it-- was the public school, the goyim, Gentile. So there was no-- really no name. It was the orthodox school, that's all. That's all as far as I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What subject-- subjects did you learn in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: What subjects? History, math, normal what the things that you learn in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in cheder what would you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cheder [NON-ENGLISH], Rashi, Gemara, of course I had kindergarten, pre-kindergarten, to learn how to daven. Don't ask me today whether I know it. I know how to daven. That's about it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many friends in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, lots of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your future goals as a young person?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a young person, as a very young I wanted to be a chazzan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that because you were-- you saw the chazzan and you liked--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I sang in the choir. And then I got interested in bicycles, motorcycles, sewing machines. You know, my father had 30 of them. And I used to clean them and fix them as a young boy. I liked to work. And I'm still doing it. I still like to work. It's just my hobby, workin'. I like to create. I like challenges. I like to build, and then look at it, that I did it. I accomplished it. And I'm proud of it. Just like I'm proud of my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about the rise of Nazism as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't believe it, that it could happen. I mean, we had such a good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: But about-- about the fact-- that idea that it was coming. Did you know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard about it, as a kid of course, in Lithuania and Poland it started much earlier than it did in-- in Hungary. And when we heard that the Jews are being deported, and the train was coming through Debrecen, through the city, I volunteered with other young boys. We took a basket, and we collected food from the people. And we met the train to give it to the people. And I could never imagine that that would happen in a city-- in a country where I was born, where we lived. Because life was so beautiful. And those days, my father was home yet. But it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you hear about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Nazis, well, I heard it in shul, most of the things you hear, you know, like they used to say, [NON-ENGLISH], you know, I heard it being said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did people find out about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to listen to the Voice of America. When it came a time when Jews were not allowed to have radios, like you couldn't employ any Gentiles. And I used to listen to it in a basement, the Voice of America.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean, after you weren't allowed to have one, you listened to it in the basement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, you know, in secret. Like I would-- they would send me out in a yard to make sure nobody is coming, you know, nobody comes in. And that's how we got-- we got the news what was going on. And until one day I remember we were planning our Purim play in school. And the Germans raided Debrecen, you know, the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same day as Purim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, like the day before, yeah. And then after that things just went bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family have a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive any newspapers in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, only what we saw on the street, you know, the-- the Hungarian newspaper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any Jewish newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there information in the Hungarian paper about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some, not not all of it, you know. About the Jews, of course, they boasted that they were deporting Jews, and Jews here, the Jews there. Jews can't do this, and you know, certain things. But very little, not-- not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're just going to change tapes now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
